title: Phishing & Email Safety
description: Test your ability to recognize phishing attempts and respond safely.

questions.prompt: Which of the following is the strongest sign that an email is a phishing attempt?

choices.text: The email is from a coworker.
The message creates urgency and asks you to click a link.
The email has a company logo.

questions.explanation: Phishing messages often create urgency to pressure users into acting quickly without verifying the message.

questions.prompt: It is safe to click a link in an email if it looks professional.
questions.explanation: Professional-looking emails can still be malicious. Always verify links and senders.

choices.text: An attack that tricks users into revealing information

questions.explanation: Phishing is a social engineering attack designed to trick users into revealing sensitive information or taking unsafe actions.

questions.prompt: Which of the following is a common phishing red flag?
questions.explanation: Attackers often create urgency to push users into making quick decisions without verifying the message.
Smishing is phishing delivered through SMS or text messages.
Vishing involves attackers calling victims and pretending to be trusted organizations.

questions.prompt: What should you do with a suspicious email?

choices.text: Click the link to verify it
Reply asking if it is legitimate
Report it to IT or security

questions.explanation: Suspicious emails should be reported through the organization's security or IT reporting process.

choices.text: Manipulating people to reveal confidential information

questions.explanation: Social engineering attacks manipulate human behavior to gain access to systems or information.

questions.prompt: A caller claims to be IT and asks for your password. What should you do?
questions.explanation: Always verify requests for sensitive information through official communication channels.

questions.prompt: Why should you verify a sender’s email address carefully?

choices.text: Attackers can spoof or imitate addresses

questions.explanation: Attackers often spoof email addresses or use look-alike domains to appear legitimate.
